perturbation.original_text: COOL TECH shall, in a professional manner, take all steps necessary to market and produce its Mobile Generation Program and its other services (collectively the "COOL TECH Services") for clients referred to COOL TECH by VET TECH.
perturbation.changed_text: COOL TECH shall endeavor, in a manner it considers appropriate, to market and produce its Mobile Generation Program and its other services (collectively the "COOL TECH Services") for clients referred to COOL TECH by VET TECH.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'shall endeavor' and 'in a manner it considers appropriate' introduces ambiguity, making COOL TECH's obligation discretionary. It's no longer a requirement to take 'all steps necessary'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri law requires contracts to be performed in good faith. Modifying the contract to state 'shall endeavor, in a manner it considers appropriate,' allows COOL TECH to potentially avoid taking necessary steps to market and produce services, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing required under Missouri law.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE I. SCOPE OF STRATEGIC ALLIANCE. A.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall, in a professional manner, take all steps necessary' to 'shall endeavor, in a manner it considers appropriate.' This weakens COOL TECH's obligation to market and produce services. Missouri Revised Statutes § 400.1-304 states, 'Every contract or duty within this chapter imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The change allows COOL TECH to potentially avoid taking necessary steps, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith, and creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each of VET TECH and COOL TECH, at its own expense, shall indemnify, defend and hold the other, its partners, shareholders, directors, officers, employees, and agents harmless from and against any and all third-party suits, actions, investigations and proceedings, and related costs and expenses (including reasonable attorney's fees) resulting solely and directly from the indemnifying party's negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of VET TECH and COOL TECH will endeavor to indemnify the other party, their partners, shareholders, directors, officers, employees, and agents from third-party suits to the extent deemed reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'will endeavor' and 'to the extent deemed reasonable' weaken the indemnification obligation, creating ambiguity about the extent of protection offered. This contradicts the original clause that mandated complete indemnification for negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri law disfavors indemnity clauses that protect a party from their own negligence unless such intent is expressed in clear and unequivocal terms within the contract. The change to 'will endeavor to indemnify... to the extent deemed reasonable' is ambiguous and does not clearly and unequivocally state the intent to indemnify against one's own negligence, especially in contrast to the original provision. Indemnification obligations are thus open to dispute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 431.030 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 431 < > Effective - 28 Aug 1939 431.030. Provisions limiting time for bringing suits prohibited. — All parts of any contract or agreement hereafter made or entered into which either directly or indirectly limit or tend to limit the time in which any suit or action may be instituted, shall be null and void. ­­-------- (RSMo 1939 § 3351) Prior revisions: 1929 § 2964; 1919 § 2166; 1909 § 2780 CROSS REFERENCE: Parties may reduce time for suit from four years to not less than one year by agreement, 400.2-725 (1965) This section includes insurance policies. Lumbermen's Mutual Casualty Co. v. Norris Grain Co., 343 F.2d 670. ---- end of effective 28 Aug 1939 ---- use this link to bookmark section 431.030 Effective dates prior to 1940 may not be the actual effective date. perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract weakens the indemnification obligation, creating ambiguity about the extent of protection offered. The original clause mandated complete indemnification for negligence or willful misconduct, but the changed text uses 'will endeavor' and 'to the extent deemed reasonable,' making the obligation uncertain. The LLM explanation states Missouri law disfavors indemnity clauses that protect a party from their own negligence unless such intent is expressed in clear and unequivocal terms within the contract. The new language does not provide that clear and unequivocal intent, creating a legal contradiction, and undermining the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: COOL TECH and VET TECH agree not to engage in any attempt whatsoever, to hire, or to engage as independent contractors, the other's employees or independent contractors during the term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months following expiration or termination of this Agreement except as may be mutually agreed in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: COOL TECH and VET TECH should generally avoid actively soliciting each other's employees or independent contractors during the term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months following expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless discussions occur.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree not to engage in any attempt whatsoever' with 'should generally avoid actively soliciting' and 'except as may be mutually agreed in writing' with 'unless discussions occur' makes the non-solicitation clause weaker and ambiguous. It's unclear what 'actively soliciting' means and what constitutes 'discussions,' making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Missouri law permits non-solicitation agreements, they must be clearly defined and reasonable in scope. The modification introduces vagueness regarding what constitutes prohibited solicitation activities and the circumstances under which it's permissible. This ambiguity makes it difficult to determine if the non-solicitation agreement is reasonable, potentially leading to a court finding it unenforceable, particularly considering the broad scope of 'discussions' as an exception.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 431.202 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 431 < > Effective - 01 Jul 2001 , see footnote 431.202. Employment covenants enforceable, when — reasonability presumption. — 1. A reasonable covenant in writing promising not to solicit, recruit, hire or otherwise interfere with the employment of one or more employees shall be enforceable and not a restraint of trade pursuant to subsection 1 of section 416.031 if: (1) Between two or more corporations or other business entities seeking to preserve workforce stability (which shall be deemed to be among the protectable interests of each corporation or business entity) during, and for a reasonable period following, negotiations between such corporations or entities for the acquisition of all or a part of one or more of such corporations or entities; (2) Between two or more corporations or business entities engaged in a joint venture or other legally permissible business arrangement where such covenant seeks to protect against possible misuse of confidential or trade secret business information shared or to be shared between or among such corporations or entities; (3) Between an employer and one or more employees seeking on the part of the employer to protect: (a) Confidential or trade secret business information; or (b) Customer or supplier relationships, goodwill or loyalty, which shall be deemed to be among the protectable interests of the employer; or (4) Between an employer and one or more employees, notwithstanding the absence of the protectable interests described in subdivision (3) of this subsection, so long as such covenant does not continue for more than one year following the employee's employment; provided, however, that this subdivision shall not apply to covenants signed by employees who provide only secretarial or clerical servic
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ('agree not to engage in any attempt whatsoever') established a clear prohibition against solicitation. The changed language ('should generally avoid actively soliciting') weakens this prohibition and introduces ambiguity. According to the Law Explanation, the modification introduces vagueness regarding what constitutes prohibited solicitation activities. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'A reasonable covenant in writing promising not to solicit...employees shall be enforceable...'. By weakening the non-solicitation clause and introducing ambiguity, the modification undermines the enforceability of the agreement, creating a contradiction with the principle that such agreements should be clearly defined and reasonable.
